>> But if it were that simple, everyone
would be doing it, right? Look,
nowadays, most programmers learn by
copying AI code and watching tutorials.
And that's fine. It works [music] to a
point at least. But if that's all you
do, you stay shallow. Tutorials teach
you what books teach you why. When you
read something like the pragmatic
programmer or fundamentals of software
architecture, you stop copy pasting
solutions [music]
and start building mental models. And
that's what separate programmers who can
solve anything from programmers who need
a tutorial for everything. Read the
actual documentation too, not just the
quick start guide. The people who know
it tool deeply are the ones that end up
enjoying programming more. But reading
alone doesn't cut it. You have to get
your hands dirty. You know that feeling
when you copy code from Claude, it
works, and you just move on? That's the
trap. The programmers who grow fast are
the ones who then ask, "Okay, but why
does this work? What happens if I remove
this one line?" They break things on
purpose. They build dumb little side
scripts just to test an idea. There's
research [music] on this called
desirable difficulties. The idea that
making learning harder on purpose leads
to stronger long-term retention. And
naturally, experimenting means you're
going to try things that feel
uncomfortable. Which brings me to this.
If you've been building the same type of
[music] app for 2 years or only using
the one language you learned first,
that's a comfort zone and it's killing
your growth. Vagotssky called the sweet
spot the zone of proximal development,
the edge of what you can currently do.
Not so hard you're lost, but hard enough
that you have to think. Take the ticket
nobody wants. [music] Try a language
you've never touched. That discomfort
you feel is your brain building new
pathways. Here's the thing, though. Most
of the time, nobody hands you the hard
problems. You have to reach for them.
Especially early in your career, you
have to go to your team lead and say, "I
want something bigger." Volunteer for
the module nobody wants to touch. Own
outcomes, not just tasks. Responsibility
is a skill like any other. And the only
way to get better at it is to actually
carry some. Once you're doing harder
work, you want real feedback on it.
That's where open- source comes in. And
I think it's the most underrated growth
tool for programmers. A lot of people
assume it's for senior engineers who
code exclusively in Voodoo. It's not.
Your first contribution can literally be
a documentation fix, but now you're
inside a real codebase. You see how
production level code is structured and
you get feedback from engineers who do
this professionally. Go to GitHub, find
a project you actually use. Look at the
issues tab. Start there. The feedback
you get from open source is powerful.
The fastest way to level up is to be
around people who have higher standards
than you. Jim Ran said, "You're the
average of the five people you spend the
most time with. If everyone around you
is fine with messy code and half shipped
features, that becomes your normal. Find
communities, Discord servers, local
meetups, people whose code you look at
and think, "I'm not there yet." You
don't need a formal mentor. You just
need people whose standards you respect.
Now, once you've learned things from
reading, experimenting, getting pushed
by better people, you need to teach.
>> What?
>> This sounds counterintuitive when you're
still learning, but it's one of the most
effective things you can do. Richard
Fineman believed that if you can't
explain something simply, you don't
actually understand it. Write a blog
post, explain a concept to a friend,
record a quick video for a classmate or
co-orker. The moment you try to teach
something, every gap in your
understanding becomes obvious. And
closing those gaps is where real
learning happens. So basically, read,
experiment, get uncomfortable, and ship
more. And if you want to streamline this
process, then Superbase, the sponsor of
today's videos, got you covered.
Superbase is a complete backend for both
web and mobile apps based on free
open-source software. It's got off
storage, a Postgrass database, real time
and edge functions out of the box for
your back end and SDKs on the front end
so you can easily connect your
infrastructure to your overengineered
front end. To start using Superbase, you

neatly arranged in an intuitive and easy
to use interface so you can easily set

Vector built right into the database. So
